docs.title: FraiseQL
docs.text:
Build high-performance GraphQL APIs with PostgreSQL views and Python type safety.

FraiseQL is a Python framework that combines the power of PostgreSQL views with GraphQL, giving you complete control over your API while leveraging your database's query optimization capabilities.

docs.title: Before FraiseQL
docs.text:
# Complex ORM queries, N+1 problems, manual optimization
users = db.query(User).options(
    joinedload(User.posts).joinedload(Post.comments)
).filter(User.active == True).all()

# Manual GraphQL resolvers for each field
@strawberry.field
async def posts(self, info) -> list[Post]:
    return await get_posts_for_user(self.id)  # N+1 query problem

docs.title: With FraiseQL
docs.text:
-- Create a PostgreSQL view that returns JSONB
CREATE VIEW v_user AS
SELECT jsonb_build_object(
    'id', u.id,
    'name', u.name,
    'posts', COALESCE(jsonb_agg(
        jsonb_build_object(
            'id', p.id,
            'title', p.title,
            'comments', p.comment_count
        )
    ) FILTER (WHERE p.id IS NOT NULL), '[]'::jsonb)
) AS data
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN posts p ON p.user_id = u.id
GROUP BY u.id;

# Define your GraphQL schema with Python types
from fraiseql import ID, FraiseQL

@fraiseql.type
class User:
    id: ID
    name: str
    posts: list[Post]

@fraiseql.query
async def users(info) -> list[User]:
    repo = info.context["repo"]
    return await repo.find("v_user")  # One query, fully optimized

docs.title: ⚡ 50-1000x Faster with Lazy Caching
docs.text:
• TurboRouter: Bypass GraphQL parsing for registered queries
  • Lazy Caching: Pre-computed responses stored in PostgreSQL
  • Sub-millisecond: Cache hits return instantly
  • Automatic invalidation: Version tracking by bounded contexts

docs.title: 🎯 Type Safety Throughout
docs.text: Modern Python typing (3.13+) ensures type safety from database to GraphQL schema.

docs.title: 🏗️ True CQRS Architecture
docs.text:
• Queries: Read from optimized views (v_ prefix) and table views (tv_ prefix)
  • Mutations: Call PostgreSQL functions (fn_ prefix)
  • Separation: Storage model evolves independently from API model
  • Bounded contexts: Clear domain boundaries with version tracking

docs.title: 🔒 Enterprise Security Built-in
docs.text: Field-level authorization, rate limiting, CSRF protection, and SQL injection prevention out of the box.

docs.title: 📦 Database-Native Caching
docs.text:
• No Redis/Memcached: Cache lives in PostgreSQL
  • Historical data: Cache becomes valuable audit trail
  • Version tracking: Automatic invalidation on data changes
  • Multi-tenant: Built-in cache isolation

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
Let's build a blog API in under 5 minutes:

# 1. Define your GraphQL types
from fraiseql import FraiseQL, ID
from datetime import datetime
from dataclasses import dataclass

app = FraiseQL(database_url="postgresql://localhost/blog")

@fraiseql.type
class Post:
    id: ID
    title: str
    content: str
    author: str
    created_at: datetime
    comment_count: int = 0

# 2. Create a PostgreSQL view
"""
CREATE VIEW v_post AS
SELECT jsonb_build_object(
    'id', p.id,
    'title', p.title,
    'content', p.content,
    'author', u.name,
    'created_at', p.created_at,
    'comment_count', COUNT(c.id)
) AS data
FROM posts p
JOIN users u ON u.id = p.user_id
LEFT JOIN comments c ON c.post_id = p.id
GROUP BY p.id, u.name;
"""

# 3. Define your GraphQL query
@app.query
async def posts(info, limit: int = 10) -> list[Post]:
    repo = info.context["repo"]
    return await repo.find("v_post", limit=limit)

# 4. Run it!
# uvicorn app:app --reload

That's it! You now have a GraphQL API with: - Type-safe schema definition - Optimized database queries - GraphQL playground for testing - Production-ready performance

docs.title: Core Philosophy
docs.text:
FraiseQL embraces Database Domain-Driven Design:

  1. PostgreSQL is your domain model - Business logic lives in functions and views
  2. Views are your API projection - Denormalized, optimized for reading
  3. Python defines your schema - Explicit type definitions and GraphQL operations
  4. One source of truth - PostgreSQL handles all data transformations

This means: - ✅ No more N+1 queries - ✅ No more complex ORM queries - ✅ No more manual query optimization - ✅ Full control over your GraphQL schema

docs.title: Who Uses FraiseQL?
docs.text: FraiseQL is perfect for teams who: - Want to leverage PostgreSQL's full power - Need high-performance GraphQL APIs - Value type safety and explicit schema definition - Are building multi-tenant SaaS applications - Want to reduce infrastructure complexity

docs.title: ⚡ Performance Features
docs.text:
• Lazy Caching - Database-native response caching
  • TurboRouter - Bypass GraphQL parsing overhead
  • Query Optimization - PostgreSQL view best practices
  • N+1 Prevention - Automatic query batching

docs.title: 🔒 Security Features
docs.text:
• Field Authorization - Fine-grained access control
  • Rate Limiting - Built-in request throttling
  • SQL Injection Prevention - Automatic query sanitization
  • CSRF Protection - Cross-site request forgery prevention

docs.title: 🏗️ Architecture Patterns
docs.text:
• CQRS Implementation - Command Query Responsibility Segregation
  • Event Sourcing - Audit trail and time-travel queries
  • Multi-tenancy - Isolated data per tenant
  • Bounded Contexts - Domain-driven design

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FraiseQL uses a comprehensive CI/CD pipeline with multiple quality gates to ensure only high-quality, tested code reaches production.

docs.title: Pipeline Architecture
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Code Push] --> B[Quality Gate]
    B --> C{All Checks Pass?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Merge Allowed]
    C -->|No| E[Merge Blocked]

    F[Tag Push] --> G[Release Pipeline]
    G --> H[Tests Required]
    G --> I[Lint Required]
    G --> J[Security Required]
    H --> K{All Pass?}
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K -->|Yes| L[Publish to PyPI]
    K -->|No| M[Release Blocked]

docs.title: 1. Quality Gate (quality-gate.yml)
docs.text:
Triggers: Push to main/dev, PRs to main/dev

Purpose: Comprehensive quality checks that must pass before merge.

Jobs: - Tests: Full test suite including core and integration tests - Lint: Code formatting and style checks (ruff) - Security: Security vulnerability scanning (bandit) - Quality Gate Summary: Aggregates results, fails if any check fails

Branch Protection: This workflow should be required for main branch protection.

docs.title: 2. Publish (publish.yml)
docs.text:
Triggers: Push to tags matching v*

Purpose: Publishes releases to PyPI with mandatory quality checks.

Critical Feature: 🚨 Publish job requires ALL quality jobs to pass 

publish:
  needs: [test, lint, security]  # Will not run if any dependency fails

Jobs: 1. Test: Full test suite (required for release) 2. Lint: Code quality checks (required for release) 3. Security: Security checks (required for release) 4. Publish: Package build and PyPI upload (only if all pass)

docs.title: 3. Test Legacy (test.yml)
docs.text:
Status: Deprecated, being phased out

Triggers: Feature branches, PRs to dev

Purpose: Backward compatibility during transition

docs.title: Tests Must Pass ✅
docs.text:
• Core test suite: pytest tests/ -m "not blog_simple and not blog_enterprise"
  • Integration tests: pytest tests/integration/examples/
  • Coverage reporting to Codecov

docs.title: Code Quality Must Pass ✅
docs.text:
• Ruff linting: ruff check .
  • Ruff formatting: ruff format --check .

docs.title: Security Must Pass ✅
docs.text: • Bandit security scanning: bandit -r src/

docs.title: Safe Release Steps
docs.text:
1. Develop on feature branch
  2. Create PR to dev → Quality gate runs
  3. Merge to dev (only if quality gate passes)
  4. Create PR to main → Quality gate runs
  5. Merge to main (only if quality gate passes)
  6. Create tag (git tag v1.0.0)
  7. Push tag (git push origin v1.0.0) → Release pipeline runs
  8. Publish only if ALL checks pass ✅

docs.title: Historical Issue (Fixed)
docs.text:
Problem: Package v0.5.3 was published despite failing tests.

Root Cause: Original publish workflow had no dependency on test success.

Evidence: 

// GitHub API response for commit 351bb8b (v0.5.3):
{"conclusion":"failure","workflowName":"Test"},      // ❌ Tests failed
{"conclusion":"success","workflowName":"Publish"},   // ✅ Published anyway!

Solution: New publish workflow requires test/lint/security to pass: 

publish:
  needs: [test, lint, security]  # 🚨 CRITICAL: Won't run if any fail

docs.title: Recommended Settings for main branch:
docs.text:
• ✅ Require status checks to pass before merging
  • ✅ Require up-to-date branches before merging
  • ✅ Required status checks:
  • quality-gate / Tests
  • quality-gate / Lint
  • quality-gate / Security
  • quality-gate / Quality Gate ✅

docs.title: Recommended Settings for dev branch:
docs.text:
• ✅ Require status checks to pass before merging
  • ✅ Required status checks:
  • quality-gate / Tests
  • quality-gate / Lint

docs.title: Check Workflow Status
docs.text:
# List recent workflow runs
gh run list --limit 10

# Check specific workflow
gh run view <run-id>

# Check failing workflow logs
gh run view <run-id> --log-failed

docs.title: Verify Release Safety
docs.text:
# Before creating a tag, ensure latest commit passes all checks
gh run list --branch main --limit 1 --json conclusion,workflowName

# Should show all workflows with "conclusion": "success"

docs.title: If Bad Release is Published
docs.text:
1. Yank from PyPI (if possible)
  2. Create hotfix branch
  3. Fix issue
  4. Follow full release process
  5. Publish patched version

docs.title: If Quality Gate Blocks Valid Code
docs.text:
1. Check workflow logs: gh run view <run-id> --log-failed
  2. Fix actual issue (don't bypass unless critical)
  3. For urgent fixes: Use admin override with justification

docs.title: For Developers
docs.text:
• ✅ Run tests locally before pushing: pytest tests/
  • ✅ Run linting locally: ruff check . && ruff format --check .
  • ✅ Check quality gate status before requesting review
  • ✅ Address all quality gate failures

docs.title: For Maintainers
docs.text:
• ✅ Never bypass quality gates without documented justification
  • ✅ Monitor failed releases and improve pipeline accordingly
  • ✅ Regularly review and update quality standards
  • ✅ Ensure branch protection rules are properly configured

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: For CI/CD pipeline issues, create an issue with the ci/cd label.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This guide covers the safety mechanisms in place to prevent broken code from reaching the repository and production.

docs.title: Layer 1: Pre-commit Hooks
docs.text:
When: Every git commit What: Runs comprehensive test suite locally Purpose: Catch issues before they enter git history

# Pre-commit hooks automatically run on every commit
git commit -m "your changes"  # Tests run automatically

# To run pre-commit hooks manually
make pre-commit

docs.title: Layer 2: Pre-push Hooks
docs.text:
When: Every git push What: Runs full test suite before push to remote Purpose: Final safety check before code reaches remote repository

# Pre-push hooks automatically run on every push
git push origin branch-name  # Tests run automatically

docs.title: Layer 3: GitHub Quality Gates
docs.text: When: PR creation, branch pushes What: CI/CD runs comprehensive test suite Purpose: Prevent broken code from reaching main/dev branches

docs.title: Layer 4: Branch Protection Rules
docs.text: When: PR merge attempts What: Requires all CI checks to pass Purpose: Enforce quality standards at repository level

docs.title: Recommended Commands
docs.text:
# 1. Safe commit workflow
make safe-commit              # Runs tests first, then prompts for commit
git add -A && git commit -m "message"

# 2. Safe push workflow
make safe-push               # Runs full tests, then prompts for push
git push origin branch-name

# 3. Quick test verification
make verify-tests            # Check current test status

docs.title: Manual Safety Checks
docs.text:
# Run core tests (fast)
make test-core

# Run full test suite (comprehensive)
make test

# Check pre-commit setup
make test-commit-safety

docs.title: ❌ What Went Wrong Previously
docs.text:
The issue occurred because:

  1. Incomplete local testing - Individual tests passed but full suite wasn't run
  2. Pre-commit bypass - Tests may have been skipped due to environment issues
  3. No pre-push verification - Code was pushed without final verification

docs.title: Enhanced Pre-commit Hook
docs.text:
• Strict error handling (set -e)
  • Clear failure messages
  • Comprehensive test run with -x flag (fail fast)
  • Environment validation (checks for uv)

docs.title: New Pre-push Hook
docs.text:
• Full test suite runs before every push
  • Blocks push if any test fails
  • Clear error reporting

docs.title: Improved Makefile Commands
docs.text:
• make safe-commit - Test before commit
  • make safe-push - Test before push
  • make verify-tests - Quick status check

docs.title: 1. Install Pre-commit Hooks
docs.text: make pre-commit-install

docs.title: 2. Verify Setup
docs.text: make test-commit-safety

docs.title: 3. Test the Protection
docs.text:
# Try committing with a failing test (should be blocked)
echo "assert False" >> tests/test_example.py
git add tests/test_example.py
git commit -m "test"  # Should fail and block commit
git restore tests/test_example.py  # Restore file

docs.title: 🚨 Emergency Bypass (Use Sparingly)
docs.text:
In rare cases where you need to bypass safety checks:

# Skip pre-commit hooks (not recommended)
git commit --no-verify -m "emergency fix"

# Skip pre-push hooks (not recommended)
git push --no-verify origin branch-name

⚠️ Warning: Only use bypass in genuine emergencies and ensure tests pass ASAP.

docs.title: Check Hook Status
docs.text:
# List installed hooks
ls -la .git/hooks/

# Test pre-commit
pre-commit run --all-files

# Check if hooks are executable
ls -l .git/hooks/pre-*

docs.title: Debug Test Failures
docs.text:
# Run tests with verbose output
make test-core -v

# Run specific failing test
pytest tests/path/to/test.py::TestClass::test_method -v

# Check test environment
make verify-tests

docs.title: For Developers
docs.text:
1. Always run make test-core before committing
  2. Use make safe-commit for important changes
  3. Check make verify-tests if unsure about test status
  4. Never bypass hooks without good reason

docs.title: For Code Reviews
docs.text:
1. Verify CI passes before reviewing
  2. Check that tests cover new functionality
  3. Ensure branch protection rules are enabled

docs.title: For Repository Maintenance
docs.text:
1. Monitor hook effectiveness
  2. Update safety mechanisms as needed
  3. Review bypass usage in git logs
  4. Keep protection documentation updated

docs.title: 🔗 Related Documentation
docs.text:
• CI/CD Pipeline Documentation
  • Quality Gate Workflow
  • Branch Protection Settings

Remember: These safety mechanisms exist to maintain code quality and prevent production issues. They're not obstacles—they're safeguards! 🛡️

docs.title: Nested Object Resolution in FraiseQL
docs.text: This guide explains how FraiseQL handles nested objects that have their own sql_source, and how to control whether they should be resolved via separate queries or use embedded data from the parent's JSONB column.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Overview
  • Default Behavior: Embedded Data
  • Explicit Nested Resolution
  • When to Use Each Approach
  • Common Patterns
  • Performance Considerations
  • Troubleshooting

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
When a GraphQL type with sql_source appears as a field in another type, FraiseQL needs to know whether to: 1. Use embedded data from the parent's JSONB column (default) 2. Make a separate query to the nested type's sql_source table

This behavior is controlled by the resolve_nested parameter in the @type decorator.

docs.title: Default Behavior: Embedded Data
docs.text: By default (resolve_nested=False), FraiseQL assumes nested objects are embedded in the parent's JSONB data column. This is the most common pattern when using PostgreSQL views that pre-join related data.

docs.title: Example: Embedded Organization in User
docs.text:
from fraiseql import type
from uuid import UUID
from typing import Optional

# Organization type - data will be embedded when nested
@type(sql_source="v_organizations")
class Organization:
    id: UUID
    name: str
    identifier: str
    status: str

# User type with embedded organization
@type(sql_source="v_users")
class User:
    id: UUID
    first_name: str
    last_name: str
    email: str
    organization: Optional[Organization] = None  # Uses embedded data

docs.title: Database View Structure
docs.text:
The view should include the nested object's data in the JSONB column:

CREATE VIEW v_users AS
SELECT
    u.id,
    jsonb_build_object(
        'id', u.id,
        'first_name', u.first_name,
        'last_name', u.last_name,
        'email', u.email,
        'organization', jsonb_build_object(  -- Embedded organization
            'id', o.id,
            'name', o.name,
            'identifier', o.identifier,
            'status', o.status
        )
    ) AS data
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN organizations o ON u.organization_id = o.id;

docs.title: GraphQL Query
docs.text:
query GetUser {
  user(id: "123") {
    firstName
    lastName
    organization {  # No separate query - uses embedded data
      name
      identifier
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• ✅ No N+1 queries - All data fetched in one query
  • ✅ No tenant_id required for nested objects
  • ✅ Better performance - Single database roundtrip
  • ✅ Simpler context - No need to pass additional parameters

docs.title: Explicit Nested Resolution
docs.text: When you need FraiseQL to make separate queries for nested objects (true relational data), set resolve_nested=True.

docs.title: Example: Department as a Relation
docs.text:
# Department will be queried separately when nested
@type(sql_source="v_departments", resolve_nested=True)
class Department:
    id: UUID
    name: str
    code: str

# Employee with department as a foreign key relation
@type(sql_source="v_employees")
class Employee:
    id: UUID
    name: str
    department_id: Optional[UUID] = None  # Foreign key
    department: Optional[Department] = None  # Will query v_departments

docs.title: Database View Structure
docs.text:
The parent view only includes the foreign key, not the full nested data:

CREATE VIEW v_employees AS
SELECT
    e.id,
    jsonb_build_object(
        'id', e.id,
        'name', e.name,
        'department_id', e.department_id  -- Only the FK, not full data
    ) AS data
FROM employees e;

-- Separate view for departments
CREATE VIEW v_departments AS
SELECT
    d.id,
    d.tenant_id,  -- May require tenant_id for multi-tenant apps
    jsonb_build_object(
        'id', d.id,
        'name', d.name,
        'code', d.code
    ) AS data
FROM departments d;

docs.title: GraphQL Query
docs.text:
query GetEmployee {
  employee(id: "456") {
    name
    department {  # Triggers separate query to v_departments
      name
      code
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Context must include necessary parameters (e.g., tenant_id)
  • Parent must have the foreign key field (e.g., department_id)
  • Nested type's sql_source must be queryable with available context

docs.title: Use Default (Embedded Data) When:
docs.text:
• ✅ Your views pre-join and embed related data
  • ✅ You want optimal performance (single query)
  • ✅ The nested data is relatively small
  • ✅ You're using JSONB columns for denormalization
  • ✅ You want to avoid N+1 query problems

docs.title: Use resolve_nested=True When:
docs.text:
• ✅ Nested data is truly relational (not embedded)
  • ✅ You need fresh data from the source table
  • ✅ The nested data is large and rarely accessed
  • ✅ You have complex authorization rules per table
  • ✅ You're migrating from a traditional ORM pattern

docs.title: Pattern 1: Multi-Tenant Application with Embedded Data
docs.text:
@type(sql_source="v_tenants")
class Tenant:
    id: UUID
    name: str
    plan: str

@type(sql_source="v_users_with_tenant")
class User:
    id: UUID
    email: str
    tenant: Tenant  # Embedded - no tenant_id issues

docs.title: Pattern 2: Large Related Data (Use Separate Queries)
docs.text:
@type(sql_source="v_documents", resolve_nested=True)
class Document:
    id: UUID
    title: str
    content: str  # Large text field
    size_bytes: int

@type(sql_source="v_projects")
class Project:
    id: UUID
    name: str
    document_ids: list[UUID]
    documents: list[Document]  # Resolved separately to avoid bloat

docs.title: Pattern 3: Mixed Approach
docs.text:
# Frequently accessed, small data - embedded by default
@type(sql_source="v_categories")
class Category:
    id: UUID
    name: str
    color: str

# Large, rarely accessed data - resolved separately
@type(sql_source="v_images", resolve_nested=True)
class Image:
    id: UUID
    url: str
    data: bytes  # Large binary data

@type(sql_source="v_products")
class Product:
    id: UUID
    name: str
    category: Category  # Embedded (small, frequent)
    images: list[Image]  # Separate queries (large, infrequent)

docs.title: Embedded Data (Default)
docs.text:
Query: { user { organization { name } } }

Execution:
1. SELECT data FROM v_users WHERE id = ?
   ↓
   Returns: { user: { organization: { name: "Acme Corp" } } }

Total queries: 1

docs.title: Nested Resolution (resolve_nested=True)
docs.text:
Query: { employees { department { name } } }  # N employees

Execution:
1. SELECT data FROM v_employees
   ↓
2. SELECT data FROM v_departments WHERE id = ? (for each unique dept)
   ↓
   Returns merged data

Total queries: 1 + number of unique departments

docs.title: Error: "missing a required argument: 'tenant_id'"
docs.text:
Cause: A nested type with sql_source is trying to resolve separately but lacks required context.

Solutions: 1. Remove resolve_nested=True if data should be embedded 2. Ensure the view includes embedded data in JSONB 3. If separate resolution is needed, provide tenant_id in context

docs.title: Error: "Cannot read property 'name' of null"
docs.text:
Cause: Expected embedded data is missing from parent's JSONB.

Solutions: 1. Update view to include nested object in JSONB 2. Use LEFT JOIN to handle optional relationships 3. Set field as Optional[Type] in Python

docs.title: N+1 Query Problems
docs.text:
Symptom: Many queries executed for a list with nested objects.

Solution: - Remove resolve_nested=True unless absolutely necessary - Ensure views embed frequently accessed nested data - Consider using DataLoader pattern for resolve_nested=True cases

docs.title: From Traditional ORM to FraiseQL
docs.text:
If migrating from an ORM that always does separate queries:

# Step 1: Start with resolve_nested=True (matches ORM behavior)
@type(sql_source="departments", resolve_nested=True)
class Department:
    ...

# Step 2: Create views with embedded data
CREATE VIEW v_employees_with_dept AS ...

# Step 3: Remove resolve_nested=True after views are ready
@type(sql_source="departments")  # Now uses embedded data
class Department:
    ...

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Default to embedded data - Better performance, simpler code
  2. Document your choice - Add comments explaining why resolve_nested=True is used
  3. Monitor query performance - Use query logging to detect N+1 problems
  4. Design views thoughtfully - Include commonly accessed nested data
  5. Use resolve_nested sparingly - Only when truly necessary

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Default behavior (resolve_nested=False): Uses embedded JSONB data
  • Explicit resolution (resolve_nested=True): Makes separate queries
  • Choose based on: Data size, access patterns, and performance needs
  • Best practice: Use embedded data unless you have a specific reason not to

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: FraiseQL provides comprehensive network operators for IP address classification and filtering. This document outlines the implemented operators and explains the design decisions behind operator selection.

docs.title: Core Operations (v0.6.0+)
docs.text:
• Basic: eq, neq, in, notin, nin
  • Subnet: inSubnet, inRange
  • Classification: isPrivate, isPublic, isIPv4, isIPv6

docs.title: Enhanced Operations (v0.7.4+)
docs.text:
• isLoopback - RFC 3330 (IPv4) / RFC 4291 (IPv6)
  • IPv4: 127.0.0.0/8

  • IPv6: ::1/128

  • isLinkLocal - RFC 3927 (IPv4) / RFC 4291 (IPv6)

  • IPv4: 169.254.0.0/16 (APIPA)

  • IPv6: fe80::/10

  • isMulticast - RFC 3171 (IPv4) / RFC 4291 (IPv6)

  • IPv4: 224.0.0.0/4

  • IPv6: ff00::/8

  • isDocumentation - RFC 5737 (IPv4) / RFC 3849 (IPv6)

  • IPv4: 192.0.2.0/24, 198.51.100.0/24, 203.0.113.0/24

  • IPv6: 2001:db8::/32

  • isCarrierGrade - RFC 6598

  • IPv4: 100.64.0.0/10 (Carrier-Grade NAT)
  • IPv6: No equivalent

docs.title: Design Decisions: Excluded Operators
docs.text: The following operators were intentionally excluded during development for the reasons listed:

docs.title: ❌ isBroadcast
docs.text: Problem: Ambiguous definition - IPv4: Only 255.255.255.255 or include subnet broadcasts? - IPv6: No broadcast concept exists - Decision: Too ambiguous to implement reliably

docs.title: ❌ isSiteLocal
docs.text: Problem: Deprecated functionality - IPv6: fec0::/10 deprecated per RFC 3879 - Decision: Should not implement deprecated standards

docs.title: ❌ isUniqueLocal
docs.text: Problem: Limited applicability - IPv6: fc00::/7 - IPv4: No equivalent - Decision: Very IPv6-specific, limited use case

docs.title: ❌ isReserved
docs.text: Problem: Too vague - Multiple ranges with unclear definition - What constitutes "reserved" varies by context - Decision: Too broad and ambiguous

docs.title: ❌ isGlobalUnicast
docs.text: Problem: Complex negative definition - Defined as "not private, not multicast, not special-use" - Complex logic prone to errors - Decision: Too complex for reliable implementation

docs.title: ❌ isnull
docs.text: Note: This is handled by NullOperatorStrategy, not NetworkOperatorStrategy

docs.title: Basic IP Classification
docs.text:
query {
  devices(where: {
    ipAddress: { isPrivate: true }
  }) {
    name
    ipAddress
  }
}

docs.title: Advanced Network Analysis
docs.text:
query {
  servers(where: {
    ipAddress: { isLoopback: false, isDocumentation: false }
  }) {
    name
    ipAddress
  }
}

docs.title: Subnet Filtering
docs.text:
query {
  devices(where: {
    ipAddress: { inSubnet: "192.168.1.0/24" }
  }) {
    name
    ipAddress
  }
}

docs.title: IPv4/IPv6 Support
docs.text: All operators support both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses where applicable. IPv4-specific operators (like isCarrierGrade) gracefully handle IPv6 addresses by returning appropriate results.

docs.title: Boolean Logic
docs.text: All classification operators accept boolean values: - true: IP address matches the classification - false: IP address does NOT match the classification

docs.title: PostgreSQL Integration
docs.text: All operators use PostgreSQL's native inet type with subnet containment (<<=) and equality operators for optimal performance and correctness.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Comprehensive test coverage ensures: - Correct SQL generation for all operators - Proper IPv4/IPv6 handling - Backward compatibility - Error handling for invalid input

See: - tests/unit/sql/test_enhanced_network_operators.py - tests/unit/sql/test_network_operator_strategy_fix.py

docs.title: Advanced Topics
docs.text: Deep technical guides for FraiseQL v0.1.0 advanced features, performance optimization, and architectural patterns.

docs.title: Performance Guide
docs.text: Complete guide to FraiseQL performance optimization including connection pooling, query optimization, caching strategies, and monitoring.

docs.title: TurboRouter
docs.text: High-performance direct SQL execution for registered queries, bypassing GraphQL parsing overhead for optimal response times.

docs.title: Lazy Caching
docs.text: Database-native caching system that stores pre-computed GraphQL responses in PostgreSQL with automatic invalidation through bounded context version tracking, achieving sub-millisecond response times while preserving valuable historical data.

docs.title: Eliminating N+1 Queries
docs.text: Strategies for preventing N+1 query problems using composable SQL views and strategic DataLoader usage.

docs.title: Performance Comparison
docs.text: Benchmarks and comparisons with other GraphQL frameworks.

docs.title: Domain-Driven Database
docs.text: Implementing domain-driven design patterns directly in PostgreSQL with CQRS views and event sourcing.

docs.title: Database API Patterns
docs.text: Best practices for designing database schemas optimized for GraphQL APIs, including view composition and JSONB strategies.

docs.title: LLM-Native Architecture
docs.text: How FraiseQL's design enables AI-assisted development with predictable patterns and self-documenting schemas.

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all FraiseQL configuration options, environment variables, and tuning parameters.

docs.title: Authentication Patterns
docs.text: Implementing authentication and authorization with Auth0, custom providers, and field-level security.

docs.title: Security Best Practices
docs.text: Production security guide covering SQL injection prevention, rate limiting, query complexity analysis, and CORS.

docs.title: Identifier Management
docs.text: Comprehensive guide to the Triple ID Pattern (internal ID, UUID primary key, business identifier) including generation strategies, recalculation patterns, lookup optimization, and migration techniques for enterprise-grade identifier management.

docs.title: Pagination Patterns
docs.text: Implementing efficient cursor-based and offset pagination with proper index usage and performance optimization.

docs.title: Subscription Architecture
docs.text: WebSocket-based real-time subscriptions with PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY and pub/sub patterns.

docs.title: JSONB Optimization
docs.text: Leveraging PostgreSQL's JSONB capabilities for flexible schemas and efficient nested data queries.

docs.title: Query Translation Pipeline
docs.text:
FraiseQL's core innovation is its efficient GraphQL to SQL translation:

graph LR
    A[GraphQL Query] --> B[Parser]
    B --> C[Analyzer]
    C --> D[SQL Generator]
    D --> E[Executor]
    E --> F[Result Mapper]
    F --> G[GraphQL Response]

docs.title: Execution Modes
docs.text:
FraiseQL automatically selects the optimal execution mode:

  1. TurboRouter with Lazy Cache
  2. Pre-computed GraphQL responses
  3. Sub-millisecond cache hits
  4. Automatic invalidation via bounded contexts

  5. Perfect for hot paths

  6. TurboRouter Direct SQL

  7. Pre-compiled SQL templates
  8. Zero GraphQL parsing overhead

  9. Fresh data every time

  10. JSON Passthrough

  11. Direct JSONB extraction
  12. Minimal transformation

  13. Ideal for view-based queries

  14. Standard Mode

  15. Full GraphQL processing
  16. Maximum flexibility
  17. Complex query support

docs.title: Connection Pool Architecture
docs.text:
# Optimal pool configuration by load
config = FraiseQLConfig(
    database_pool_size=50,      # Base connections
    database_max_overflow=20,   # Burst capacity
    database_pool_timeout=10,   # Fast failure
)

Sizing Guidelines: - Small apps: 10-20 connections - Medium apps: 20-50 connections - Large apps: 50-100 connections - Enterprise: 100+ with read replicas

docs.title: Type System Performance
docs.text:
FraiseQL's type system is optimized for:

  • Zero-copy deserialization: Direct PostgreSQL to JSON
  • Lazy field resolution: Only compute requested fields
  • Batch loading: Automatic N+1 prevention
  • Type caching: Schema built once at startup

docs.title: Performance Estimates
docs.text: Note: The following performance figures are estimates provided for guidance. Actual performance will vary based on your hardware, database design, query complexity, and deployment configuration. We recommend conducting your own benchmarks for production planning.

docs.title: Estimated Query Latencies (p95)
docs.text: Query Type FraiseQL (est.) Typical ORM-based (est.) Simple fetch < 5ms 10-50ms Nested 3-level < 10ms 50-200ms List (100 items) < 20ms 100-500ms Mutations < 10ms 20-100ms

docs.title: Estimated Throughput Capability
docs.text:
Scenario Estimated Range Simple queries 1,000-10,000 req/s Complex queries 500-5,000 req/s Mixed workload 800-8,000 req/s

Estimates based on a 4-core server with optimized PostgreSQL configuration.

docs.title: Resource Usage Estimates
docs.text: Metric Estimated Range Base memory 40-60MB Per connection 1-3MB Per 1000 req/s 100-200MB additional

docs.title: Why Direct SQL?
docs.text:
FraiseQL generates SQL directly rather than using an ORM because:

  1. Performance: No ORM overhead or abstraction penalty
  2. Predictability: SQL output is deterministic and optimizable
  3. PostgreSQL Features: Full access to JSONB, CTEs, window functions
  4. Query Planning: Leverage PostgreSQL's optimizer directly

docs.title: Why Views Over Joins?
docs.text:
FraiseQL recommends composable views because:

  1. Single Query: Entire object graph in one round trip
  2. Index Usage: Views can have optimized indexes
  3. Maintainability: Business logic in database
  4. Performance: PostgreSQL optimizes view queries

Example: 

-- Composable view eliminates N+1
CREATE VIEW v_user_with_stats AS
SELECT
    u.*,
    jsonb_build_object(
        'post_count', COUNT(p.id),
        'comment_count', COUNT(c.id),
        'last_login', MAX(l.created_at)
    ) as stats
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN posts p ON p.author_id = u.id
LEFT JOIN comments c ON c.user_id = u.id
LEFT JOIN login_history l ON l.user_id = u.id
GROUP BY u.id;

docs.title: Why JSONB for Nested Data?
docs.text:
1. Atomic Fetches: Complete objects in single column
  2. Index Support: GIN indexes for fast queries
  3. Flexibility: Schema evolution without migrations
  4. Performance: Native PostgreSQL optimization

docs.title: SQL Injection Prevention
docs.text:
FraiseQL uses multiple layers of protection:

  1. Parameterized Queries: All values use placeholders
  2. Type Validation: Strong typing prevents injection
  3. Query Analysis: AST validation before execution
  4. Allowlist Mode: TurboRouter only executes known queries

docs.title: Query Complexity Analysis
docs.text:
# Automatic complexity scoring
config = FraiseQLConfig(
    complexity_enabled=True,
    complexity_max_score=1000,
    complexity_max_depth=10
)

Prevents: - Deeply nested queries - Expensive list operations - Cartesian products - Resource exhaustion

docs.title: Field-Level Authorization
docs.text:
@fraise_type
class User:
    id: UUID          # Public
    email: str        # Public

    @field
    @requires_role("admin")
    def ssn(self) -> str:
        return self._ssn  # Admin only

    @field
    @requires_auth
    def private_notes(self) -> str:
        return self._notes  # Authenticated only

docs.title: Built-in Metrics
docs.text:
FraiseQL provides Prometheus-compatible metrics:

  • fraiseql_query_duration_seconds
  • fraiseql_query_complexity_score
  • fraiseql_db_pool_connections
  • fraiseql_cache_hit_ratio
  • fraiseql_turbo_router_hits

docs.title: Performance Profiling
docs.text:
# Enable detailed timing
config = FraiseQLConfig(
    include_execution_metadata=True,
    enable_request_logging=True
)

Response includes: 

{
  "data": { ... },
  "extensions": {
    "execution_time_ms": 1.2,
    "mode": "turbo",
    "complexity_score": 15,
    "db_queries": 1
  }
}
